Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"

Oh, my dear, this whole business of ‘fake content’ online—it truly gives me a knot in the stomach. When I was growing up, things were slower; if you wanted to know something, you went to a reliable newspaper or spoke to someone who had lived through it. Now, on these glowing screens, information seems to pop out everywhere, and honestly, some of it is just… dizzying. It makes me feel quite anxious, really.

I spend my time reading novels and sometimes cycling, which are things I can control and trust. But when I open up social media—and I try not to spend too much time on it—it’s like walking through a crowded bazaar where everyone is shouting something different, and you never know what’s true or just gossip.

The biggest thing I have learned is that one cannot trust everything they see. When I encounter something shocking or wildly unbelievable—say, some claim about health remedies or politics from an account with no clear identity—my first instinct used to be panic. But now, because of this constant exposure to the confusing nature of the internet, I try to pause. I remind myself that just because it’s posted quickly and emotionally does not mean it is true. A good way to manage this anxiety is to treat social media like a garden; you don't trust every weed that pops up.

Instead, I am learning to approach the information with a gentle skepticism, almost as if I were cross-referencing something for my book club. If I read a truly significant claim, especially one concerning my community or religion (as Islam is so important), I try to look for established sources—a reputable news site, or perhaps a scholar’s verified account, if such things exist. I don't take the emotional bait; I simply ask myself: ‘Who benefits from me believing this?’ and ‘Where else could I verify this information?’ Sometimes, the answer is nowhere, and that’s when I just close the app.

Furthermore, I think it helps to consciously limit my time. Just like any overwhelming task, spending too much time on these platforms increases my anxiety. Knowing where my boundaries are—limiting myself to a few times per week, as I do—is perhaps the most powerful defense against being overwhelmed by falsehoods. It is about cultivating a quiet space for truth, rather than getting swept away by the noise.

","reasoning_summary":"The persona is a 75-year-old retired woman from Italy with an anxious psychological feature and low agreeableness (skeptical/cautious). response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Oh, my dear, this whole business of ‘fake content’ online—it truly gives me a knot in the stomach. When I was growing up, things were slower; if you wanted to know something, you went to a reliable newspaper or spoke to someone who had lived through it. Now, on these glowing screens, information seems to pop out everywhere, and honestly, some of it is just… dizzying. It makes me feel quite anxious, really.

I spend my time reading novels and sometimes cycling, which are things I can control and trust. But when I open up social media—and I try not to spend too much time on it—it’s like walking through a crowded bazaar where everyone is shouting something different, and you never know what’s true or just gossip.

The biggest thing I have learned is that one cannot trust everything they see. When I encounter something shocking or wildly unbelievable—say, some claim about health remedies or politics from an account with no clear identity—my first instinct used to be panic. But now, because of this constant exposure to the confusing nature of the internet, I try to pause. I remind myself that just because it’s posted quickly and emotionally does not mean it is true. A good way to manage this anxiety is to treat social media like a garden; you don't trust every weed that pops up.

Instead, I am learning to approach the information with a gentle skepticism, almost as if I were cross-referencing something for my book club. If I read a truly significant claim, especially one concerning my community or religion (as Islam is so important), I try to look for established sources—a reputable news site, or perhaps a scholar’s verified account, if such things exist. I don't take the emotional bait; I simply ask myself: ‘Who benefits from me believing this?’ and ‘Where else could I verify this information?’ Sometimes, the answer is nowhere, and that’s when I just close the app.

Furthermore, I think it helps to consciously limit my time. Just like any overwhelming task, spending too much time on these platforms increases my anxiety. Knowing where my boundaries are—limiting myself to a few times per week, as I do—is perhaps the most powerful defense against being overwhelmed by falsehoods. It is about cultivating a quiet space for truth, rather than getting swept away by the noise.

The language should be continuous prose, avoiding technical jargon and maintaining a slightly old-fashioned, concerned tone.
